title: Man and Sin
html:
Big Question

If God created us out of love, why is there so much pain and evil in the world?

«If God exists and is good, why do children suffer?» The question is thrown by Ivan Karamazov in the most profound novel ever written about the problem of evil: Fyodor Dostoevsky's The Brothers Karamazov. Ivan is no ordinary atheist. He is an intellectual who has seen the suffering of the innocent — children tortured, mothers weeping, lives shattered by human cruelty — and says: it is not that he does not believe in God; he is returning his ticket to a world where this is possible.

His brother Alyosha, the young and pure monk, listens in silence. He has no philosophical answer to give him. But Dostoevsky, who had spent four years in a Siberian prison camp and had seen evil up close, suggests his answer in the figure of the elder Zosima: evil is not answered with arguments, but with love that takes on the suffering of the other. «There is so much power in active love,» Zosima says. «Do not seek the meaning of suffering; bear it with others.»

The Catechism does not dodge this question. It states clearly that original sin is a reality — a wound in our nature that inclines us toward evil — but that it does not have the last word (CCC 385-390). The last word belongs to Christ, who enters into human suffering and redeems it from within. Dostoevsky understood: the answer is not a theory that explains evil, but a Presence that passes through it.

The Long Defeat and the Sudden Joy

J.R.R. Tolkien, the author of The Lord of the Rings, was a devout Catholic who understood sin not as an abstract doctrine but as the deep structure of every human story. He coined two concepts that illuminate the mystery of the Fall and redemption with rare power: the long defeat and eucatastrophe.

The long defeat is the experience of humanity after the Fall. History seems like a losing battle: every generation inherits the wounds of the one before; every victory against evil is partial and temporary; every garden has a serpent lurking. Tolkien's Elves in The Silmarillion embody this tragedy: immortal beings who watch everything they love fade and decay, fighting a war against Morgoth — the original rebel, the Satan-figure whose pride introduced discord into the very music of creation — knowing they can never fully win. It is the experience Saint Paul named when he cried out, «I do not do what I want, but I do the very thing I hate» (Rom 7:15). We are caught in a long defeat, generation after generation.

But Tolkien also gave us eucatastrophe — a word he invented from Greek roots meaning «the good catastrophe,» the sudden joyous turn when all seems lost. At the climax of The Lord of the Rings, when Frodo succumbs to the Ring's power and claims it for himself, when the quest has failed and evil seems to triumph, Gollum bites the Ring from Frodo's finger and falls into the fire. Evil destroys itself. The impossible happens. Tolkien called this «a sudden and miraculous grace: never to be counted on to recur.»

The Protoevangelium — God's promise in Genesis 3:15 that the woman's offspring would crush the serpent's head — is the original eucatastrophe. In the very moment of the Fall, when humanity had just chosen death, God spoke a promise of victory. And the Cross and Resurrection are the ultimate eucatastrophe: when sin had done its worst, when God himself lay dead in a tomb, the sudden joyous turn — the stone rolled away, the grave empty, death defeated from within. The long defeat is real, but it is not the end of the story. «Where sin increased, grace abounded all the more» (Rom 5:20).

Going Deeper

Tolkien's Ainulindalë — the creation myth that opens The Silmarillion — is a profound meditation on Genesis. Ilúvatar (God) composes a great musical theme, and the Ainur (angelic beings) sing it into being. But Melkor, the most powerful of the Ainur, introduces discord — his own proud variations — into the music. Each time, Ilúvatar weaves Melkor's discord into a new, more beautiful theme. Finally, Ilúvatar declares: «And thou, Melkor, shalt see that no theme may be played that hath not its uttermost source in me, nor can any alter the music in my despite. For he that attempteth this shall prove but mine instrument in the devising of things more wonderful.» Evil is real, but God's providence is greater. Even sin is woven, mysteriously, into a story of redemption. This is exactly what the Church means by the felix culpa — the «happy fault» that merited so great a Redeemer.

Created in the Image of God

The human being is not an accident of cosmic evolution. The Bible opens with a declaration that gives every person infinite dignity: «Then God said, ‘Let us make humankind in our image, according to our likeness’» (Gen 1:26). Unlike the sun, the stars, the animals, humanity bears a unique resemblance to the Creator. We are not merely creatures; we are sons and daughters.

What does «image of God» mean concretely? The Catechism identifies three essential capacities: reason (we can know truth), freedom (we can choose the good), and above all love (we can give ourselves to another). We are God's image because we are capable of entering into a personal relationship with him — of knowing him, of responding to him freely, of loving him (CCC 356-358). An animal can sense, react, even bond; but only a human being can pray, can worship, can say «I love you» to God and mean it with full awareness.

But the image of God in us is not merely individual. God is Trinity — a communion of Persons — and we were created for communion. The human being is not made for solitude. «It is not good that the man should be alone» (Gen 2:18). We were created to live in relationship: with God, with others, and with all creation. Our dignity does not depend on what we have or achieve, but on what we are: children of God, made for love (CCC 357-360).

Activity

Look at your hands. Touch them. Now think: what can your hands do that makes you like God? They can caress, build, write, embrace, create. The image of God is not abstract: it shows in what you can do out of love. Write down three things you can do today that reflect that you are made in God's image. Then ask yourself: who needs my hands today?

Body and Soul

The human being is a unity of body and soul. We are not a spirit trapped in a body, nor a body without a spirit. We are both at once, inseparably. The Catechism states it clearly: «The human person, created in the image of God, is a being at once corporeal and spiritual» (CCC 362). The body is not a prison or an obstacle; it is an essential part of who we are. That is why the resurrection of the body is not a fantasy: God saves us whole, body and soul.

Connection

Read Psalm 8:5-6: «What are human beings that you are mindful of them? You have made them a little lower than angels, and crowned them with glory and honor.» The psalmist marvels that God cares about us. Have you stopped to think that God knows you by name, not by number? What does it mean to you that your body also shares in the dignity of being God's image — that your physical existence is not an afterthought but part of his good creation?

The spiritual soul is what makes us capable of freedom, knowledge, and love. It is not something added to the body; it is the principle that animates it and gives it unity. When the Bible says that God «breathed into his nostrils the breath of life» (Gen 2:7), it is telling us that human life comes directly from God, unlike other creatures. The human being is dust and spirit at the same time: humility and greatness together (CCC 362-365). And because every human soul is created immediately by God, every human being — regardless of ability, stage of development, or circumstance — possesses inviolable dignity.

Male and Female: Equal Dignity, Complementary Gifts

God created man and woman with equal dignity. «In the image of God he created them; male and female he created them» (Gen 1:27). Sexual difference is not an accident or a hierarchy; it is part of God's design. Man and woman are equal in dignity and different in complementarity. Together they reflect the image of God in a way that neither can reflect alone (CCC 369-373). The fact that the human being exists as male and female is not a biological footnote; it reveals something about God himself, who is a communion of love.

Context

In a world that often confuses equality with uniformity, the Christian faith proposes something different: equal dignity and different gifts. We are not interchangeable; we are complementary. Sin has distorted this complementarity — the domination of man over woman is a consequence of the Fall, not of God's original design (Gen 3:16). Christ restores the original dignity of both. In him, as Saint Paul says, «there is no longer male and female; for all of you are one in Christ Jesus» (Gal 3:28) — not abolishing difference, but healing its distortion into hierarchy.

The Fall: "Did God Really Say?"

If the human being was created so good, how did evil enter the world? Genesis answers with a narrative of terrifying psychological realism. The scene is the garden of Eden — paradise, harmony, intimacy with God. But there is another presence in the garden. «Now the serpent was more crafty than any other wild animal that the Lord God had made» (Gen 3:1). The serpent is not just a snake; it is the mask of the devil, the fallen angel who had already chosen rebellion against God and now seeks to drag humanity into his revolt (CCC 391-395).

The serpent does not attack. He does not threaten. He simply asks a question. «Did God say, ‘You shall not eat from any tree in the garden’?» (Gen 3:1). Notice the distortion. God had not said that. God had said: «You may freely eat of every tree of the garden; but of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil you shall not eat» (Gen 2:16-17). The serpent takes God's generous permission and reframes it as oppressive prohibition. He takes the one tree that is off-limits and makes it seem like the only tree that matters. This is temptation's oldest strategy: it makes God seem like a rival to our freedom rather than its source.

The woman replies, correcting the serpent — but she also adds to God's word: «God said, ‘You shall not eat of the fruit of the tree that is in the middle of the garden, nor shall you touch it, or you shall die’» (Gen 3:3). God never said «nor shall you touch it.» The serpent does not need to make us deny God's word; he only needs to make us add to it or subtract from it. Distortion is enough.

Then the serpent goes for the kill: «You will not die; for God knows that when you eat of it your eyes will be opened, and you will be like God, knowing good and evil» (Gen 3:4-5). The accusation is devastating: God is holding out on you. God's commandment is not protection but manipulation. You can be your own god. This is the core of original sin: not merely breaking a rule, but wanting to be like God without God — to determine for yourself what is good and what is evil, to be the ultimate authority over your own life (CCC 396-398).

So the woman «saw that the tree was good for food, and that it was a delight to the eyes, and that the tree was to be desired to make one wise» (Gen 3:6). She took the fruit and ate. She gave some to her husband, who was with her, and he ate. Then their eyes were opened — not to divine wisdom, but to shame. «They knew that they were naked; and they sewed fig leaves together and made loincloths for themselves» (Gen 3:7).

Then comes one of the most haunting moments in all of Scripture. «They heard the sound of the Lord God walking in the garden at the time of the evening breeze, and the man and his wife hid themselves from the presence of the Lord God among the trees of the garden» (Gen 3:8). They hide. They, who moments before walked with God in intimacy, now cower among the trees. And God calls out: «Where are you?» (Gen 3:9).

This question echoes through all of human history. God knows where they are. He is not asking for information. He is asking them to recognize where they now stand — separated, ashamed, afraid, lost. «Where are you?» is the question God asks every sinner, every generation, every human heart that has chosen autonomy over love. It is not the question of an angry judge; it is the question of a Father who has lost his children and wants them back.

The man answers: «I heard the sound of you in the garden, and I was afraid, because I was naked; and I hid myself» (Gen 3:10). Fear has replaced trust. Shame has replaced intimacy. And when God asks, «Have you eaten from the tree?» the blame-shifting begins. The man blames the woman: «The woman whom you gave to be with me, she gave me fruit from the tree, and I ate.» The man even implicitly blames God: «the woman you gave me.» The woman blames the serpent: «The serpent tricked me, and I ate» (Gen 3:11-13). Sin shatters communion in every direction: with God, with each other, with oneself, with creation.

Original Sin: A Broken Communion

The Church's teaching on original sin is often misunderstood. It is not simply «Adam and Eve ate a piece of fruit and now we are all punished.» The Catechism presents original sin as something far deeper: a state of broken communion — transmitted not by imitation but by propagation (CCC 404).

What does this mean? Original sin is not spread because we copy Adam and Eve's bad example, like a child learning bad habits from parents. It is transmitted through human nature itself — through the very condition into which every human being is born. Like a hereditary disease that passes from generation to generation, original sin is a wound in human nature that we inherit, not a personal guilt that we incur. We are not personally responsible for Adam's sin, but we suffer its consequences. We are born into a world and a human nature that is already broken (CCC 402-406).

Saint Augustine, who thought more deeply about sin than perhaps any other theologian, expressed it with brutal honesty: «Factus sum et dissipatus sum» — «I was made and I unmade myself.» We were not created broken; we broke ourselves. Evil is not a substance that God created; it is a privation, a distortion of the good. The devil, who sinned first, tempted man; but man sinned freely. And the Fall is not merely a distant historical event. It recurs — in a different but real sense — every time we choose our own will over God's love (CCC 391-398).

Sin is not just «breaking rules.» Sin is breaking relationship — with God, with others, with creation, and with our own deepest self. The serpent's temptation always follows the same script: make God's love seem like a restriction, make autonomy seem like freedom, make the forbidden fruit seem like the only thing worth having. And the result is always the same: shame instead of intimacy, hiding instead of communion, fear instead of trust.

The Catechism identifies the essence of the first sin as disobedience born of pride: man, tempted by the devil, «let his trust in his Creator die in his heart and, abusing his freedom, disobeyed God's command» (CCC 397). He preferred himself to God. He wanted to be «like God» but «without God, and not in accordance with God» (CCC 398). This is the root from which every other sin grows: the refusal to accept our creaturely dependence on a God who made us for love.

The Consequences: Concupiscence and the Wounded Nature

Adam and Eve's sin had devastating consequences. Harmony was broken in every direction. The relationship with God was fractured: instead of walking with him in the cool of the garden, man hides. The relationship between man and woman was distorted: domination replaced communion (Gen 3:16). The relationship with creation was altered: work became toil, the ground produces thorns (Gen 3:17-19). Death entered the human story: «you are dust, and to dust you shall return» (Gen 3:19). And most profoundly: human nature itself was wounded (CCC 400-405).

Not destroyed. The image of God was not erased. The human being is still capable of goodness, beauty, and love. But the wound is real. Our intellect was darkened — we find it harder to know the truth. Our will was weakened — we find it harder to choose the good. And a new inward tendency arose: concupiscence, the inclination toward sin that remains even after baptism, the disordered desire that pulls us toward what we know is wrong (CCC 405, 2514-2516).

Concupiscence is not sin itself, but it is the tinder for sin — the interior drag toward evil that makes temptation so powerful. Saint Paul describes it with raw honesty that is painfully familiar to every human being: «I do not understand my own actions. For I do not do what I want, but I do the very thing I hate» (Rom 7:15). «I can will what is right, but I cannot do it. For I do not do the good I want, but the evil I do not want is what I do» (Rom 7:18-19). Does that sound familiar? We all know that experience: we know what is right, but we do the opposite. That inner civil war is concupiscence — the wound of original sin playing out in daily life.

Saint Paul takes the diagnosis even deeper in Romans 3:23: «All have sinned and fall short of the glory of God.» The universal scope of sin is not a reason for despair but an honest acknowledgment of our common condition. And David, in the great penitential Psalm 51, traces sin back to its root: «Indeed, I was born guilty, a sinner when my mother conceived me» (Ps 51:5). This is not an excuse — «I was born this way, so it is not my fault» — but a recognition of depth: sin is not just bad behavior; it is a condition that infects the whole person and requires a remedy greater than mere willpower.

God's warning to Cain captures the dynamic of sin with terrifying precision: «Sin is lurking at the door; its desire is for you, but you must master it» (Gen 4:7). Sin is not passive; it desires us. It hunts us. And we must master it — but we cannot do so by willpower alone. We need grace.

Activity

Imagine a broken mirror. The pieces still reflect the light, but the image is distorted. That is original sin: the image of God in us is still there, but it is wounded. Now imagine someone repairing the mirror with love and patience — polishing each shard, fitting them back together, restoring the reflection. Who is that someone? What is his name? Draw or journal what the restored mirror looks like — and what still needs mending.

The Promise: The Protoevangelium

But God did not stand by with folded arms. Immediately after the Fall, in the midst of pronouncing the consequences of sin, God spoke a promise that changes everything. To the serpent he said: «I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your offspring and hers; he will strike your head, and you will strike his heel» (Gen 3:15).

I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your offspring and hers; he will strike your head, and you will strike his heel. — Genesis 3:15

Theologians call this verse the Protoevangelium — the «first Gospel,» the first proclamation of good news. In the very moment humanity fell, God promised a Redeemer. The woman whose offspring would crush the serpent's head is Mary, the new Eve; her offspring is Christ, the new Adam (CCC 410-411). The serpent will strike his heel — a foreshadowing of the Cross, where Christ would be wounded — but he will crush the serpent's head — the definitive victory over sin and death.

This promise is not a footnote. It is the seed from which the entire story of salvation grows. Every covenant, every prophet, every promise in the Old Testament is an unfolding of Genesis 3:15. It reaches its fulfillment at the Cross, where the woman's Son crushes the serpent's head through his own death and resurrection. And it reaches its icon in Revelation 12, where the woman clothed with the sun stands victorious over the dragon.

Where Sin Increased, Grace Abounded More

Saint Paul draws the great contrast between Adam and Christ in Romans 5:12-21. Sin entered the world through one man, and death through sin. But grace enters through another man, Jesus Christ. And Paul insists that the grace is greater than the sin:

«If the many died through the one man's trespass, much more surely have the grace of God and the free gift in the grace of the one man, Jesus Christ, abounded for the many» (Rom 5:15). «Where sin increased, grace abounded all the more» (Rom 5:20).

This is the fundamental asymmetry of the Christian faith: sin is real, but grace is more real. Evil is powerful, but God's mercy is more powerful. The wound is deep, but the remedy is deeper still. The Church's faith does not minimize sin; it magnifies grace. The darkness of the Fall only makes the light of redemption shine brighter. This is why the Church sings in the Exsultet at the Easter Vigil: «O happy fault that earned so great, so glorious a Redeemer!» (O felix culpa).

The history of salvation does not begin in the New Testament. It begins in Genesis. God did not leave us alone in our misery. He pursued us from the very moment we hid from him. «Where are you?» is not a question of condemnation; it is the voice of a Father who will not rest until his children come home (CCC 411-421).

Biblical Connection: Two Gardens, Two Women, Two Answers

Read Genesis 3:1-15 alongside Luke 1:26-38. In the garden of Eden, a woman listens to a serpent and says «no» to God. In the garden of Nazareth (tradition places the Annunciation in a house with a garden), a woman listens to an angel and says «yes.» Eve's no brought sin and death into the world. Mary's yes brought the Savior who conquers sin and death. The Church Fathers called Mary «the new Eve» — not because she was sinless by nature (original sin affected her too, which is why she needed a Savior), but because she was preserved from sin by a singular grace: the Immaculate Conception. Where Eve was deceived, Mary trusted. Where Eve grasped at divinity, Mary surrendered to God's will. «Let it be with me according to your word» (Lk 1:38).

And just as Adam's disobedience made all sinners, Christ's obedience makes all righteous who are united to him. The symmetry of salvation is perfect: a tree in the garden brought death; a tree on Calvary brought life. The first Adam was tempted in a garden and fell; the new Adam was tempted in a garden (Gethsemane) and conquered. The story of sin is always the same; but the story of grace is always greater.

For Reflection

  1. The serpent's first move was to distort God's word: «Did God really say...?» What «serpents» whisper to you today that God's love is restrictive or that his commandments are oppressive? How can you recognize and resist that voice?
  2. Saint Paul cries out: «I do not do what I want, but I do the very thing I hate» (Rom 7:15). In what specific area of your life do you experience that tension most sharply right now? Name it honestly before God.
  3. God's first question to fallen humanity was not «What have you done?» or «How could you?» but «Where are you?» If God asked you that question today, what would you answer? Where are you — hiding, ashamed, running, or turning back toward him?
  4. Tolkien's «long defeat» names the experience of sin across generations. Where do you see the long defeat playing out — in your family, your community, or your own heart? And where have you glimpsed the eucatastrophe — the sudden, unexpected turn of grace?
  5. The Protoevangelium (Gen 3:15) promises that the serpent's head will be crushed. In what area of your life do you most urgently need Christ to crush the serpent's head — to break a pattern of sin, heal a wound, or restore a broken relationship?

Key Idea: We were created in God's image for communion. We fell through pride, breaking that communion and wounding human nature. Sin is not just breaking rules but breaking relationship — with God, with others, with ourselves. Yet God did not wait. In the very moment of the Fall, he promised a Redeemer. The long defeat is real, but the eucatastrophe is certain: where sin increased, grace abounded all the more. The story of humanity is the story of a Father searching for his children, calling through the garden: «Where are you?» — and then coming himself, in Christ, to bring them home.

Cultural Resources

John Milton, Paradise Lost (1667) — The greatest epic poem in the English language is a vast meditation on Genesis 3. Milton's Satan is perhaps literature's most compelling villain — proud, charismatic, self-deceived — and his famous declaration, «Better to reign in Hell than serve in Heaven,» captures the essence of sin as the refusal of creaturely dependence. But Milton also gives us Adam, who after the Fall, when shown the future of humanity by the archangel Michael — war, murder, corruption — is told that a Redeemer will come. Adam's response is one of the most beautiful expressions of the felix culpa in literature: «O goodness infinite, goodness immense! / That all this good of evil shall produce, / And evil turn to good.» Read Book XII for the full sweep.

J.R.R. Tolkien, The Silmarillion (1977) — The «Ainulindalë» (Music of the Ainur) is Tolkien's Genesis. Ilúvatar (God) composes a theme; the Ainur (angels) develop it; but Melkor, the greatest of them, introduces discord out of pride — he wants to create his own theme, independent of Ilúvatar. Each time Melkor's discord rises, Ilúvatar weaves it into a new, more beautiful music. Finally Ilúvatar declares that even Melkor's rebellion will prove to be «but mine instrument in the devising of things more wonderful.» This is the theological heart of The Lord of the Rings: good and evil are not equal opposing forces; evil is parasitic, and even its worst is ultimately woven into a greater good. The ring is destroyed not by Frodo's strength but by a mysterious convergence of mercy (Frodo's pity for Gollum) and providence (Gollum's fall).

Michelangelo, The Fall and the Expulsion from Eden (Sistine Chapel, 1508-1512) — One of the nine central panels on the Sistine Chapel ceiling shows, in a single composition, the Temptation on the left and the Expulsion on the right. The tree stands in the center. On the left, Eve reaches for the fruit from the serpent's hand (Michelangelo paints the serpent with a woman's torso, a tradition going back to medieval art). Adam also reaches, his gesture almost greedy. On the right, an angel with a sword drives them out. The contrast is shocking: Eve and Adam, on the expulsion side, are hideous — contorted, aged, their faces masks of terror and despair. The beauty of the first panel is answered by the horror of the second. Sin makes us ugly. Grace alone restores beauty. Stand before this image (even in reproduction) and ask: which side do I inhabit today?

The Virgen de Guadalupe as the Woman Crushing the Serpent — The image of Our Lady of Guadalupe, imprinted on the tilma of San Juan Diego in 1531, is rich with theological symbolism. The Virgin stands on a crescent moon, her foot resting on a dark angel's head. For the indigenous peoples of Mexico, the serpent (coatl) was the central symbol of the dark gods and the religion of human sacrifice. The tilma proclaims silently what Genesis 3:15 promised: the woman whose offspring crushes the serpent's head has come. Mary stands victorious over the powers of darkness. And she comes not as a conquering European but as a mestiza, with indigenous features, speaking Nahuatl to a humble native man. The evangelization of the Americas is inseparable from Guadalupe: she is the woman who says to every Mexican, every indigenous heart, «Am I not here, I who am your mother?» The serpent's head is crushed not by force but by maternal love.

William Friedkin, The Exorcist (1973) — Adapted from William Peter Blatty's novel (itself based on a documented case), The Exorcist is one of the most serious cinematic treatments of evil ever made. The film does not treat demonic possession as mere psychological disorder; it takes the reality of the Enemy seriously. Father Karras, the young Jesuit psychiatrist, begins as a skeptic — he has lost his faith, he doubts the existence of the devil — but in the face of irreducible evil, he rediscovers the necessity of Christ. The film reminds us that the devil is not a metaphor. As the Catechism teaches: «The power of Satan is, nonetheless, not infinite. He is only a creature, powerful from the fact that he is pure spirit, but still a creature. He cannot prevent the building up of God's reign» (CCC 395). The Exorcist does not glorify evil; it testifies that evil is real and that Christ is the only answer to it. Father Karras's final act — offering himself to the demon to save the girl — is a layman's echo of the Cross.

Saint Augustine, The Confessions (397-400 AD) — The most personal book in the history of theology. Augustine recounts his own fall and redemption with psychological depth unmatched in ancient literature. The famous theft of the pears (Book II): he did not need the fruit; he stole it for the sheer pleasure of doing wrong, and he did it with friends — «I loved my own perishing,» he writes. The addictions, the search for love in wrong places, the intellectual pride. And then, the grace: a child's voice chanting «take and read,» the random opening of Romans 13:13-14, and the conversion that changed Western civilization. The Confessions is Romans 7 and 8 lived out in a single soul: the struggle with sin, and the victory of grace. It is the living proof that where sin increased, grace abounded all the more.
